title: Results
text:
Quantitative results

Figure: Examples of generated masks. First row: original cropped RGB image. Second row: SAMv2.1 Small AMG masked image. Third row, model-generated SAM masks

title: Results
text:
Interpretation
SAM achieves lower CE than the other visual token modalities in this setup. On synthetic CLEVR scenes, this is plausible because segmentation masks are structurally simpler than RGB texture fields or continuous geometric signals.

title: Method
text: The project extends multiCLEVR with SAM-derived instance segmentation tokens. Each RGB scene is pseudo-labeled with SAM 2.1, filtered for stable masks, converted to normalized mask crops, and then tokenized with the pretrained 4M SAM tokenizer.

title: Method
text: The project extends multiCLEVR with SAM-derived instance segmentation tokens. Each RGB scene is pseudo-labeled with SAM 2.1, filtered for stable masks, converted to normalized mask crops, and then tokenized with the pretrained 4M SAM tokenizer.

title: Method
text:
Tokenization
Each segmentation instance is represented with coordinates, bounding-box information, and mask tokens. To avoid collisions with the vocabularies used by the other modalities, SAM token IDs are stored in a dedicated offset range.

FIgure: Tokenization pipeline for train sample 20

title: Method
text:
Labeling
Extending the model with segmentation instances instances requires an accurately segmented and tokenized dataset aligned with the already present modalities. For this purpose, we extend the multiCLEVR dataset from EPFL’s COM-304 course, which is composed of 50’000 train and 5’000 validation images, with 10 augmentation each.
To compute our modality tokens, we pseudo-labeled the RGB dataset using Meta Research’s SAM 2.1, extracting instance masks, and subsequently tokenizing them using the pre-trained 4M model tokenize.

Figure: Examples of tokenized masks. First row: original cropped RGB image. Second row: SAMv2.1 Small AMG masked image. Third row, tokenized & detokenized mask

Figure: Number of instance masks per image

title: Method
text:
Training setup
The reported 5-modality model is trained jointly from scratch. All encoder and decoder parameters are initialized together, and SAM is treated as an additional masked prediction target within the same multimodal framework.

title: Method
text:
Dataset structure

50,000 training scenes.
5,000 validation scenes.
10 augmentations per scene.
One aligned file per modality per sample.


A figure of the labeling/tokenization pipeline.
A compact diagram of the architecture.
A short explanation of padding, truncation, and sequence length choices.

title: Overview
text: A COM-304 project extending nano4M with SAM segmentation tokens as a fifth modality. The project asks a simple question: can a compact multimodal model learn segmentation tokens jointly with RGB, depth, normals, and text in a unified masked modeling framework?

title: Overview
text:
Key takeaways

SAM tokens can be integrated into nano4M without changing the core model architecture.
The model is trained from scratch, not warm-started from a previous checkpoint.
The “offset” in this project refers to the token index range in the data representation, not offset training.
SAM reaches competitive loss relative to the other visual modalities on synthetic CLEVR scenes.

title: Overview
text:
What was added
The extension adds a SAM-based segmentation modality to the existing multiCLEVR pipeline. RGB scenes are pseudo-labeled with SAM 2.1, converted into instance masks, normalized into fixed-length token sequences, and loaded alongside the original modalities.

title: Overview
text:
Why the offset matters
The SAM-related “offset” is purely a data indexing convention: SAM token IDs are assigned a dedicated range so they do not collide with other modality vocabularies. It does not mean the model was offset-trained or fine-tuned from an already converged checkpoint.

title: Overview
text:
What this website shows
This site complements the report with a shorter narrative, result snapshots, and a clearer explanation of what the extension adds to nano4M. It is designed to be self-contained, so a reader can understand the high-level idea without opening the PDF first.

title: Report and submission
text:
After finalizing our project “SAM x Nano4M”, we wanted to highlight our work by creating this simple website and showcase our findings and experiments.
You can find the final report here
